title: Astronomy
intro: Questions on the Astronomy unit cluster heavily around extragalactic and high-energy phenomena alongside the solar system's familiar bodies. Quasars are the single most frequent answerline, and neutron stars, gamma-ray bursts, supermassive black holes, and pulsars all recur often, reflecting how much of the canon draws on compact objects and the extreme physics of accretion. Solar system topics remain a steady presence throughout — Jupiter, Uranus, Venus, Mars, Neptune, comets, asteroids, and sunspots all appear regularly, usually keyed to a distinguishing physical feature or moon.

Stellar astrophysics forms another dense cluster: star formation, luminosity, main-sequence evolution, nucleosynthesis, and the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram recur constantly, often paired with specific stellar remnants like white dwarfs and Type Ia supernovae. Cosmology contributes a smaller but conceptually central group of answers — the cosmic microwave background, cosmic inflation, dark matter, gravitational lensing and waves, and the Big Bang itself — probing the large-scale structure and history of the universe rather than individual objects.

Exoplanets, astrobiology, and observational technique account for a further slice of the canon: detection methods like radial velocity and transits, systems such as TRAPPIST-1 and Gliese 581, and instruments from the Hubble and James Webb Space Telescopes to Gaia and WMAP. Historical and contemporary astronomers appear as common-link answers tying together their signature discoveries — Hubble and the expanding universe, Kepler and planetary motion, Chandrasekhar and the white dwarf mass limit, Vera Rubin and dark matter — while a broad layer of physical concepts (redshift, the Doppler effect, magnetic fields, general relativity) supplies the vocabulary connecting all of the above.

sections.name: Solar System
sections.blurb: Planets, moons, and small bodies orbiting the Sun.

entries.note: Largest planet in the solar system, a gas giant with the Great Red Spot storm and the four large Galilean moons.
Icy small bodies that develop comas and tails on approaching the Sun, originating in the Kuiper belt or Oort cloud.
Ice giant planet with an extreme roughly 98-degree axial tilt, discovered by William Herschel in 1781.
Small rocky bodies orbiting the Sun, concentrated mainly in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.
Second planet from the Sun, with a thick CO2 atmosphere causing a runaway greenhouse effect and surface temperatures over 460 C.
Fourth planet from the Sun, the "Red Planet," home to the solar system's largest volcano, Olympus Mons.
Outermost ice giant planet, discovered via its gravitational effect on Uranus; orbited by the moon Triton.
Temporary dark regions on the Sun's photosphere caused by magnetic flux concentrations, following an 11-year cycle.
Saturn's largest moon, with a thick nitrogen atmosphere and lakes of liquid methane and ethane.
Saturn's icy moon with active water-ice geysers erupting from its south pole, suggesting a subsurface ocean.
Neptune's largest moon, likely a captured Kuiper belt object with a retrograde orbit and cryovolcanic activity.
Largest object in the asteroid belt, classified as a dwarf planet.
The Sun's outermost atmospheric layer, visible during total solar eclipses and far hotter than the photosphere.
Disk of icy bodies beyond Neptune's orbit, home to Pluto and the source of many short-period comets.
Ringed gas giant planet, second largest in the solar system, with the moons Titan and Enceladus.
Pluto's largest moon, massive enough relative to Pluto that the pair orbit a common barycenter outside Pluto.
Smallest and innermost planet, with extreme temperature swings and a perihelion precession explained by general relativity.
Dwarf planet in the Kuiper belt, formerly the ninth planet, with its largest moon Charon.
Our solar system's G-type main-sequence star, the source of nearly all energy reaching Earth.
Stony meteorites containing chondrules, among the most primitive, unaltered material from the early solar system.
Eruption of volatile materials like water or ammonia from icy moons such as Enceladus and Triton.
Jupiter's icy moon believed to harbor a subsurface liquid water ocean beneath its cracked ice shell.
Jupiter's largest moon and the largest moon in the solar system, bigger than Mercury, with its own magnetic field.
Hypothesized period of intense asteroid and comet impacts on the inner planets roughly 4 billion years ago.
Fragments of asteroids or other bodies that survive passage through Earth's atmosphere to reach the surface.
Hypothesized spherical shell of icy bodies far beyond the Kuiper belt, source of long-period comets.
Process by which planets change their orbital distance over time through interactions with a protoplanetary disk or other bodies.
Disks of debris and ice orbiting a planet, most prominently around Saturn.
Large bodies orbiting a star that have cleared their orbital neighborhood, per the IAU definition.
Stream of charged particles continuously flowing outward from the Sun's corona.
Largest object in the asteroid belt, the first asteroid discovered and now classified as a dwarf planet.
Luminous displays in a planet's upper atmosphere caused by charged solar wind particles interacting with its magnetic field.
Earth's only natural satellite, likely formed from debris after a giant impact with a Mars-sized body, Theia.
Large planets composed primarily of hydrogen and helium, including Jupiter and Saturn.
Jupiter's innermost large moon, the most volcanically active body in the solar system due to tidal heating.
Geochemical component of some lunar rocks rich in potassium, rare earth elements, and phosphorus.
Hypothesized spherical shell of icy bodies far beyond the Kuiper belt, source of long-period comets.
Gravitational interaction where orbiting bodies exert regular periodic influence, locking their orbital periods into simple ratios.
Artificial objects such as defunct satellites and fragments orbiting Earth.
Hypothesized Mars-sized protoplanet that collided with early Earth, forming the Moon.
Force on a rotating small body caused by anisotropic thermal radiation, slowly altering its orbit.

sections.name: Stars & Stellar Evolution
sections.blurb: The life cycles of stars, from protostars through the main sequence to giant phases.

entries.note: Process by which stars condense from collapsing clouds of gas and dust within molecular clouds.
Outflow of charged particles from a star's outer layers, driving mass loss especially in giant and massive stars.
Stage of stable hydrogen-fusing stars forming a diagonal band on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram.
Process by which elements are formed via nuclear fusion in stars or in the early universe.
Zodiac constellation containing the Pleiades and Hyades star clusters, the bright star Aldebaran, and the Crab Nebula.
Massive, hot, evolved stars losing mass rapidly via strong stellar winds, precursors to some supernovae.
Process by which matter falls onto a star, black hole, or protoplanetary disk, releasing gravitational energy.
Balance between inward gravity and outward pressure that maintains a star's stable structure.
Rotating disks of matter spiraling inward onto a star, black hole, or protoplanet, releasing energy through friction.
Pulsating stars whose period directly relates to luminosity, used as standard candles for measuring cosmic distances.
Energy transport mechanism via bulk motion of plasma, occurring in stellar envelopes and cores.
Region of the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram where stars pulsate, including Cepheids and RR Lyrae variables.
Self-luminous spheres of plasma fusing hydrogen, and eventually heavier elements, in their cores.
Two stars gravitationally bound and orbiting a common center of mass.
Systems of two stars gravitationally bound and orbiting a common center of mass.
Substellar object too low in mass to sustain stable hydrogen fusion, bridging giant planets and true stars.
Hypothesized first generation of stars formed from pristine, metal-free gas after the Big Bang.
Early stage of star formation where a collapsing gas cloud has not yet begun stable hydrogen fusion.
Evolved, expanded, cooled stars that have exhausted core hydrogen and begun shell fusion.
Nuclear fusion reaction combining three helium nuclei into carbon in the cores of evolved stars.
Bright, nearby A-type star that served as a historical photometric standard and will be the pole star around 13,000 CE.
Red supergiant star in Orion nearing the end of its life, expected to explode as a supernova.
Systems of two stars gravitationally bound and orbiting a common center of mass.
Substellar objects too low in mass to sustain stable hydrogen fusion.
Evolved giant stars with more carbon than oxygen in their atmospheres, giving them a deep red color.
Sudden ignition of helium fusion in the degenerate core of a low-mass red giant.
Plot of stellar luminosity versus temperature, revealing the main sequence and giant and dwarf branches.
Plot of stellar luminosity versus temperature, revealing the main sequence and giant and dwarf branches.
Opacity-driven process causing stellar pulsations in variable stars like Cepheids.
Clouds of gas and dust in space, sites of star formation or remnants of stellar death.
Loosely bound groups of young stars formed from the same molecular cloud.
Glowing shells of gas ejected by low-to-medium mass stars near the end of their lives, leaving a white dwarf core.
Evolutionary stage where a star expands and cools after exhausting core hydrogen, fusing hydrogen in a shell.
Gravitationally bound groups of stars, either loose open clusters or dense globular clusters.
Stars whose brightness fluctuates over time due to pulsation, eclipses, or eruptive events.

sections.name: Stellar Remnants & Compact Objects
sections.blurb: White dwarfs, neutron stars, black holes, and the supernovae that produce them.

entries.note: Extremely dense remnants of core-collapse supernovae, composed almost entirely of neutrons; observed as pulsars when rotating.
Rapidly rotating neutron stars emitting beams of radiation observed as regular pulses.
Regions of spacetime with gravity so strong not even light can escape, formed from collapsed massive stars or larger processes.
Supernova remnant from a star that exploded in 1054 CE, observed by Chinese astronomers; houses the Crab Pulsar.
Explosive deaths of massive stars or accreting white dwarfs, briefly outshining entire galaxies and seeding heavy elements.
Dense remnant core of a low-to-medium mass star left after it sheds its outer layers as a planetary nebula.
Thermonuclear explosions of white dwarfs exceeding the Chandrasekhar limit, used as standard candles to measure cosmic distances.
Dense remnant cores of low-to-medium mass stars supported by electron degeneracy pressure.
Sudden implosion of a massive star's iron core that triggers a Type II supernova.
Extremely dense remnant of a core-collapse supernova, composed almost entirely of neutrons.
Rotating neutron stars detected via regular pulses of radio emission.
Explosive death of a massive star or accreting white dwarf, briefly outshining an entire galaxy.
Explosive deaths of massive stars or accreting white dwarfs.

sections.name: Galaxies & Large-Scale Structure
sections.blurb: Galaxies, clusters, and the cosmic web that organizes matter on the largest scales.

entries.note: Smooth, ellipsoidal galaxies with little gas or ongoing star formation, composed largely of older stars.
Galaxies with a flattened, rotating disk and spiral arms of gas, dust, and young stars, like the Milky Way.
Two irregular dwarf galaxies orbiting the Milky Way, visible from the Southern Hemisphere.
Dense, roughly spherical collections of old stars orbiting in a galaxy's halo.
Nearest large spiral galaxy to the Milky Way, on a collision course with it in several billion years.
Massive gravitationally bound systems of stars, gas, and dark matter, ranging from dwarf to giant elliptical and spiral forms.
Largest gravitationally bound structures in the universe, containing hundreds to thousands of galaxies bound by dark matter.
Collisions and combinations of galaxies over cosmic time, often triggering starbursts and reshaping galactic structure.
Region of the sky obscured by the Milky Way's disk, hiding extragalactic objects behind dust and stars.
Vast, roughly empty regions of space between filaments of galaxies in the cosmic web.
Roughly spherical region surrounding a galaxy's disk containing old stars, globular clusters, and dark matter.
Thread-like large-scale structures of galaxies forming the cosmic web, separated by voids.
Largest satellite galaxy of the Milky Way, site of supernova 1987A.
Our home barred spiral galaxy, containing the solar system within one of its spiral arms.
Supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way, imaged directly by the Event Horizon Telescope.
Galaxy with a flattened, rotating disk and spiral arms of gas, dust, and young stars.
Thread-like large-scale structures of galaxies forming the cosmic web.
Processes by which galaxies assembled from primordial density fluctuations and dark matter halos.
Massive gravitational anomaly pulling the Milky Way and nearby galaxies toward it, obscured by the Zone of Avoidance.
Galaxy group containing the Milky Way, Andromeda, and dozens of smaller galaxies.
Our home barred spiral galaxy containing the solar system.
Largest known globular cluster orbiting the Milky Way, possibly the remnant core of a dwarf galaxy.
Spread of velocities among stars or galaxies in a system, used to estimate mass via gravitational dynamics.

sections.name: Active Galaxies & High-Energy Phenomena
sections.blurb: Quasars, blazars, and the extreme energetics of accreting supermassive black holes.

entries.note: Extremely luminous active galactic nuclei powered by supermassive black holes accreting matter; among the most distant observable objects.
Extremely energetic explosions, the brightest electromagnetic events known, linked to collapsing massive stars or neutron star mergers.
Black holes of millions to billions of solar masses found at the centers of most galaxies, powering active galactic nuclei.
Compact, extremely luminous regions at galactic centers powered by accretion onto a supermassive black hole.
Active galactic nuclei with a relativistic jet pointed nearly directly at Earth, showing rapid variability.
Extremely luminous active galactic nucleus powered by accretion onto a supermassive black hole.
Brief, intense pulses of radio emission from extragalactic sources, of still-debated origin.

sections.name: Cosmology
sections.blurb: The origin, expansion, and large-scale evolution of the universe as a whole.

entries.note: Relic thermal radiation from about 380,000 years after the Big Bang, key evidence for the hot Big Bang model.
Unseen mass inferred from galactic rotation curves and gravitational lensing that does not interact via electromagnetism.
Theorized period of exponential expansion in the universe's first fraction of a second, explaining its flatness and uniformity.
Bending of light by massive objects predicted by general relativity, used to detect dark matter and distant galaxies.
Epoch when ultraviolet radiation from the first stars and galaxies ionized neutral hydrogen throughout the universe.
Event marking the origin of the universe's expansion from a hot, dense state roughly 13.8 billion years ago.
Standard cosmological model combining a cosmological constant (dark energy) with cold dark matter to explain cosmic structure and expansion.
Abbreviation for the cosmic microwave background radiation, relic heat from the early universe.
Stretching of light to longer wavelengths due to the expansion of the universe or relative motion; used to measure cosmic distances.
Mysterious component driving the accelerating expansion of the universe, inferred from Type Ia supernova observations.
Production of light elements, hydrogen, helium, and some lithium, in the first few minutes after the Big Bang.
Term in Einstein's field equations representing a constant energy density of space, now associated with dark energy.
Period when radiation from the first stars and galaxies ionized the neutral hydrogen filling the universe.
Increase in distance between distant galaxies over time, discovered by Hubble and now accelerating due to dark energy.
Absorption feature in distant quasar spectra indicating neutral hydrogen in the intergalactic medium before reionization.
Rate of the universe's expansion, relating a galaxy's recession velocity to its distance.
Rate of the universe's expansion, relating a galaxy's recession velocity to its distance.
A galaxy's motion relative to the smooth Hubble flow of cosmic expansion, caused by local gravitational effects.

sections.name: Exoplanets & Astrobiology
sections.blurb: Planets beyond the solar system and the search for life elsewhere.

entries.note: Planets orbiting stars other than the Sun, detected via transit photometry, radial velocity, and direct imaging.
Method of detecting exoplanets by measuring a star's wobble via Doppler shifts in its spectrum.
Planets orbiting stars other than the Sun.
Method of detecting exoplanets by observing periodic dimming as a planet passes in front of its star.
Red dwarf star hosting multiple exoplanets, some once considered potentially within its habitable zone.
Hypothesis that life or its building blocks can spread between planets or star systems.
Ultra-cool red dwarf star hosting seven roughly Earth-sized planets, several within its habitable zone.

sections.name: Observational Astronomy & Instruments
sections.blurb: Telescopes, spacecraft, and the techniques used to observe the sky.

entries.note: Long-wavelength electromagnetic radiation used via radio telescopes to study pulsars, the CMB, and galactic structure.
Radio emission line from neutral hydrogen's hyperfine transition, used to map galactic structure and the early universe.
Infrared space telescope launched in 2021, successor to Hubble, studying early galaxies and exoplanet atmospheres.
High-energy electromagnetic radiation used to study black holes, neutron stars, and hot gas in galaxy clusters.
European Space Agency mission mapping precise positions, distances, and motions of over a billion stars.
Electromagnetic radiation used to observe cool objects, dust-obscured star formation, and distant redshifted galaxies.
Optical/UV/infrared space telescope launched in 1990, producing landmark deep-field images and precision distance measurements.
Spectral emission line of hydrogen used to detect and study distant galaxies and the intergalactic medium.
NASA infrared space telescope that studied cool objects like brown dwarfs, dusty disks, and distant galaxies.
NASA satellite that mapped the cosmic microwave background with high precision, refining cosmological parameters.
Graphs of an object's brightness over time, used to detect transits, variable stars, and supernovae.
NASA spacecraft that performed the first flyby of Pluto in 2015 and later the Kuiper belt object Arrokoth.
Long-wavelength electromagnetic radiation studied via radio telescopes to probe pulsars, galaxies, and the CMB.
Branch of astronomy observing celestial objects via radio-wavelength emission.

sections.name: Astronomers & Physicists
sections.blurb: The scientists whose discoveries and namesakes define the field.

entries.note: American astronomer who showed galaxies lie beyond the Milky Way and discovered the universe's expansion via Hubble's Law.
American astronomer whose galaxy rotation curve observations provided key evidence for dark matter.
German astronomer who formulated three laws of planetary motion describing elliptical orbits around the Sun.
Japanese astrophysicist known for the Hayashi track describing the pre-main-sequence contraction of protostars.
Indian-American astrophysicist who derived the mass limit above which a white dwarf cannot support itself against collapse.
Italian astronomer who used the telescope to discover Jupiter's four largest moons and support heliocentrism.
William Herschel, discoverer of Uranus and infrared radiation, or the Herschel Space Observatory that studied cool infrared sources.
German-British astronomer who discovered Uranus in 1781 and infrared radiation.

sections.name: Physical Concepts
sections.blurb: The physical quantities and processes — mass, temperature, gravity, radiation — that underlie astronomical phenomena.

entries.note: Total energy a star radiates per unit time; combines with temperature via the Stefan-Boltzmann law to place stars on the HR diagram.
Ripples in spacetime from accelerating massive objects like merging black holes, first directly detected by LIGO in 2015.
Proportion of a star's or galaxy's mass composed of elements heavier than hydrogen and helium.
Second most abundant element in the universe, produced in Big Bang nucleosynthesis and by stellar fusion.
Color associated with hot, high-temperature stars per Wien's law and blackbody radiation.
Fundamental astronomical measurement determined via parallax, standard candles, or redshift.
Most abundant element in the universe, fuel for stellar fusion via the proton-proton chain and CNO cycle.
Determines a star's color and spectral classification; central axis of the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram.
Fine particles of solid material in interstellar space, contributing to the extinction and reddening of starlight.
Fundamental property determining a star's fate, luminosity, and lifetime.
Highest-energy electromagnetic radiation, produced in processes like supernovae, pulsars, and gamma-ray bursts.
Key element produced via the triple-alpha process in stellar cores, essential for organic chemistry.
Shift in observed wavelength due to relative motion, used to measure radial velocities and redshift.
Matter, gas and dust, that fills the space between stars within a galaxy.
Field generated by moving charges, shaping phenomena from sunspots and stellar winds to pulsar emission and galactic structure.
Differential gravitational forces that stretch bodies, responsible for tides, tidal locking, and tidal heating of moons.
Rate and direction of an object's motion, measured via Doppler shifts or proper motion.
Unit of distance equal to the average Earth-Sun distance, about 150 million kilometers.
High-energy particles, mostly protons, arriving from space and originating from sources like supernovae.
Often refers to the universe's unseen components, dark matter and dark energy.
Layer of a planet's atmosphere ionized by solar radiation, affecting radio wave propagation.
Heaviest element commonly produced by stellar fusion; its buildup in a star's core signals an imminent core-collapse supernova.
Five points in a two-body system where gravitational and centrifugal forces balance, allowing stable spacecraft positions.
Light element produced in Big Bang nucleosynthesis, with an observed abundance discrepancy from predictions.
Apparent angular movement of a star across the sky over time, relative to more distant background objects.
Helium nuclei, emitted in radioactive decay and involved in stellar nucleosynthesis reactions.
Indicator of a star's surface temperature, measured via color indices on the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram.
Fine solid grains in the interstellar or protoplanetary medium that absorb and scatter starlight.
Einstein's theory describing gravity as the curvature of spacetime by mass and energy.
Fundamental force of attraction between masses, governing orbits, stellar structure, and cosmic evolution.
The two lightest and most abundant elements in the universe, produced primarily in the Big Bang.
Process by which atoms lose or gain electrons, key to spectral classification and the epoch of reionization.
Simple hydrocarbon found in the atmospheres of giant planets and moons like Titan.
Nearly massless, weakly interacting particles produced in nuclear fusion and supernovae.
Measure of how strongly matter absorbs radiation, governing energy transport within stars.
Force per unit area that, along with gravity, determines stellar structure and hydrostatic equilibrium.
Color associated with cool, low-temperature stars per Wien's law.
Spin of a celestial body about its axis, affecting shape, magnetic field generation, and pulsar timing.
Fundamental property of a star determining its luminosity, temperature, and evolutionary fate.
Differential gravitational force stretching a body, responsible for tides and tidal heating.
Periodic rise and fall of ocean levels caused by differential gravitational pull, mainly from the Moon and Sun.
